Ultrafast decoupling of polarization and strain in ferroelectric BaTiO3subscriptBaTiO3\mathrm{BaTiO_{3}}roman_BaTiO start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 3 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT

Le Phuong Hoang European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Max Planck Institute for the Structure and Dynamics of Matter, 22761 Hamburg, Germany Institute of Experimental and Applied Physics, Kiel University, 24098 Kiel, Germany David Pesquera Catalan Institute of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (ICN2), CSIC and BIST, Campus UAB, Bellaterra, 08193 Barcelona, Spain Gerard N. Hinsley Deutsches Elektronen-Synchrotron DESY, 22607 Hamburg, Germany Robert Carley European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Laurent Mercadier European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Martin Teichmann European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Saptam Ganguly Catalan Institute of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (ICN2), CSIC and BIST, Campus UAB, Bellaterra, 08193 Barcelona, Spain Teguh Citra Asmara European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Giacomo Merzoni Dipartimento di Fisica, Politecnico di Milano, 20133, Milano, Italy European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Sergii Parchenko European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Justine Schlappa European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Zhong Yin European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany José Manuel Caicedo Roque Catalan Institute of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (ICN2), CSIC and BIST, Campus UAB, Bellaterra, 08193 Barcelona, Spain José Santiso Catalan Institute of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (ICN2), CSIC and BIST, Campus UAB, Bellaterra, 08193 Barcelona, Spain Irena Spasojevic Department de Física, Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, 08193 Bellaterra, Spain Cammille Carinan European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Tien-Lin Lee Diamond Light Source Ltd., Didcot, OX110DE Oxfordshire, United Kingdom Kai Rossnagel Institute of Experimental and Applied Physics, Kiel University, 24098 Kiel, Germany Ruprecht Haensel Laboratory, Deutsches Elektronen-Synchrotron DESY, 22607 Hamburg, Germany Jörg Zegenhagen Diamond Light Source Ltd., Didcot, OX110DE Oxfordshire, United Kingdom Gustau Catalan Catalan Institute of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (ICN2), CSIC and BIST, Campus UAB, Bellaterra, 08193 Barcelona, Spain Institucio Catalana de Recerca i Estudis Avançats (ICREA), 08010 Barcelona, Catalonia Ivan A. Vartanyants Deutsches Elektronen-Synchrotron DESY, 22607 Hamburg, Germany Andreas Scherz European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany Giuseppe Mercurio European XFEL, 22869 Schenefeld, Germany giuseppe.mercurio@xfel.eu
Abstract

A fundamental understanding of the interplay between lattice structure, polarization and electrons is pivotal to the optical control of ferroelectrics. The interaction between light and matter enables the remote and wireless control of the ferroelectric polarization on the picosecond timescale, while inducing strain, i.e., lattice deformation. At equilibrium, the ferroelectric polarization is proportional to the strain, and is typically assumed to be so also out of equilibrium. Decoupling the polarization from the strain would remove the constraint of sample design and provide an effective knob to manipulate the polarization by light. Here, upon an above-bandgap laser excitation of the prototypical ferroelectric BaTiO3, we induce and measure an ultrafast decoupling between polarization and strain that begins within 350 \fstimes350\fs350\text{\,}\fsstart_ARG 350 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG end_ARG, by softening Ti-O bonds via charge transfer, and lasts for several tens of picoseconds. We show that the ferroelectric polarization out of equilibrium is mainly determined by photoexcited electrons, instead of the strain. This excited state could serve as a starting point to achieve stable and reversible polarization switching via THz light. Our results demonstrate a light-induced transient and reversible control of the ferroelectric polarization and offer a pathway to control by light both electric and magnetic degrees of freedom in multiferroics.

Ferroelectric materials are characterized by many properties, including piezoelectricity and pyroelectricity, besides ferroelectricity, which make them attractive for a wide range of applications, such as nonvolatile memories, transistors, sensors, and actuators[1, 2]. The key property of a ferroelectric material is the ability to switch its spontaneous polarization in response to an external electric field. This is typically achieved by a static or pulsed electric field with the consequent limitations given by the need for complex circuitry and switching times of hundreds of picoseconds to nanoseconds[3]. These challenges can be overcome by optical control of the ferroelectric polarization. Light-matter interaction enables remote and wireless control of the ferroelectric polarization on the picosecond timescale[3]. Moreover, since all ferroelectrics are also piezoelectrics, the ferroelectric polarization is strongly coupled to the strain, i.e., the lattice deformation[4]. Optical control of polarization and strain has been achieved in several cases. For example, in multilayers of ferroelectric and electrode thin films, an optical laser was used to excite the metal (or semiconductor) layer and indirectly the ferroelectric material, leading to a transient modification of the strain[5, 6, 7, 8], or the polarization by charge redistribution at the interface[9]. In other studies, light was absorbed directly by the ferroelectric material, inducing changes in the spontaneous polarization[10, 11] or lattice strain in clamped[12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18] or freestanding[19] ferroelectric thin films. THz light was employed to rotate[20] or even transiently reverse the orientation of the spontaneous polarization [21]. In all these studies so far, either the polarization or the strain was measured, and a direct proportionality between spontaneous polarization and strain was typically assumed[22]. This proportionality is based on the piezoelectric effect, which is well captured by the Landau-Ginzburg-Devonshire theory when the lattice distortion is along the polarization axis[23]. While this assumption is valid under equilibrium conditions, as demonstrated experimentally, e.g. in Refs.[24, 25], it may not hold under out-of-equilibrium conditions following light-matter interaction. Decoupling the polarization from the strain would remove the constraint of sample design to achieve specific properties [24, 26], and, at the same time, would provide a more effective and ultrafast knob to manipulate the polarization by light.

To explore this scenario, we probe the out-of-plane strain and the spontaneous polarization of the prototypical ferroelectric BaTiO3 upon above-bandgap absorption of ultrashort UV light pulses (Figure 1a). A fundamental understanding of the relationship between strain and ferroelectric polarization out of equilibrium requires their investigation on their natural timescale encompassing ≈\approx≈ 100 fstimes100femtosecond100\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}start_ARG 100 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG to several tens of  pstimesabsentpicosecond\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG. We employ a combination of time-resolved X-ray diffraction (tr-XRD), time-resolved optical second harmonic generation (tr-SHG), and time-resolved optical reflectivity (tr-refl) to obtain the magnitudes and the separate dynamics of the out-of-plane lattice parameter, the spontaneous polarization, and the photoexcited carrier density, respectively[27], with a time resolution of ≈90 fsabsenttimes90femtosecond\approx$90\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$≈ start_ARG 90 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG. In this paper, we will show the mechanisms that govern the structure and polarization changes in a ferroelectric material and their complex relationship out of equilibrium in the presence of photoexcited electrons and lattice deformation. In particular, since the strain wave propagates at the speed of sound, whereas electronic interactions are much faster, we induce and measure an ultrafast decoupling between polarization and strain, which we assign to the photoexcited electrons. First, we present the lattice response to the absorption of UV laser pulses and the corresponding data modeling. Next, we present tr-SHG and tr-refl data. Finally, we bring together all the results and discuss the underlying physical mechanisms in the context of hitherto known phenomena taking place in ferroelectric materials.

Results

Photoinduced structural dynamics

Our sample consists of a coherently strained, monodomain BaTiO3 (BTO) thin film, grown on a GdScO3 (GSO) substrate, with a SrRuO3 (SRO) bottom electrode sandwiched in between (see Methods). Under a compressive strain of −0.55%percent0.55-0.55\%- 0.55 % imposed by the substrate, the BTO film shows an out-of-plane ferroelectric polarization Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT pointing toward the sample surface (Figure 1a). The sample is excited above the BTO band gap Eg=3.4 eVsubscript𝐸𝑔times3.4electronvoltE_{g}=$3.4\text{\,}\mathrm{eV}$italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 3.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_eV end_ARG[28] using 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG laser pulses at an incident pump laser fluence of 2.7 mJ cm−2times2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter22.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG. Time-resolved X-ray diffraction of the (001) Bragg reflection is employed to probe the lattice response of our ferroelectric thin film along the out-of-plane direction. The lattice deformations along the in-plane directions on the picosecond timescale are negligible, given the large ratio between photoexcited area (330×240330240330\times 240330 × 240  µ⁢m2timesabsentmicrometer2\text{\,}{\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolMicro m}}^{2}start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG start_ARG 2 end_ARG end_ARG) and BTO film thickness dBTO=34.5 nmsubscript𝑑BTOtimes34.5nanometerd_{\mathrm{BTO}}=$34.5\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 34.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG.

We observe an initial reduction of the tetragonal distortion, which goes hand in hand with lattice compression, then followed by lattice expansion. In particular, Figure 1b shows the (001) diffraction intensity IXRDsubscript𝐼XRDI_{\mathrm{XRD}}italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT of BTO as a function of the photon energy Eνsubscript𝐸𝜈E_{\nu}italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ν end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and at different pump-probe delays t𝑡titalic_t from −14 pstimes-14picosecond-14\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}start_ARG - 14 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG to 32.5 pstimes32.5picosecond32.5\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}start_ARG 32.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG. At t=2.6 ps𝑡times2.6picosecondt=$2.6\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t = start_ARG 2.6 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG we observe the following changes to the Bragg peak as compared to the ground state (at t=−14 ps𝑡times-14picosecondt=$-14\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t = start_ARG - 14 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG): a decrease in the diffraction intensity IXRDsubscript𝐼XRDI_{\mathrm{XRD}}italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT near the peak center, and a shift to higher photon energy, which implies a decrease in the out-of-plane lattice parameter c𝑐citalic_c, i.e., lattice compression (see gray curve in Figure 1b). To further explore this initial structural dynamics, we measure the delay dependence of Δ⁢IXRD/IXRD,0=[IXRD⁢(t)−IXRD,0]/IXRD,0Δsubscript𝐼XRDsubscript𝐼XRD0delimited-[]subscript𝐼XRD𝑡subscript𝐼XRD0subscript𝐼XRD0\Delta I_{\mathrm{XRD}}/I_{\mathrm{XRD,0}}=[I_{\mathrm{XRD}}(t)-I_{\mathrm{XRD% ,0}}]/I_{\mathrm{XRD,0}}roman_Δ italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = [ italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_t ) - italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ] / italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, which quantifies the relative change of IXRD⁢(t)subscript𝐼XRD𝑡I_{\mathrm{XRD}}(t)italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_t ) at the photon energy Eν=1525 eVsubscript𝐸𝜈times1525electronvoltE_{\nu}=$1525\text{\,}\mathrm{eV}$italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ν end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 1525 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_eV end_ARG of the BTO peak with respect to the equilibrium value IXRD,0subscript𝐼XRD0I_{\mathrm{XRD,0}}italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT at negative delays. We observe a maximum diffraction intensity drop of ≈4%absentpercent4\approx 4\%≈ 4 % at t=3.5 ps𝑡times3.5picosecondt=$3.5\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t = start_ARG 3.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG, with up to 99%percent9999\%99 % recovery to the equilibrium value at t≈7 ps𝑡times7picosecondt\approx$7\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t ≈ start_ARG 7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG (Figure 1c and Figure S5). We assign the initial 4%percent44\%4 % drop and recovery in diffraction intensity to the displacements of atoms within the BTO unit cell (inset of Figure 1c). Specifically, simulations based on the dynamical theory of diffraction (Figure S6) exclude the Debye-Waller effect and show that a decrease in the displacement ΔTi−OsubscriptΔTiO\Delta_{\mathrm{Ti-O}}roman_Δ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_Ti - roman_O end_POSTSUBSCRIPT between the Ti atom and the center of the O octahedron by 8 pmtimes8picometer8\text{\,}\mathrm{pm}start_ARG 8 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_pm end_ARG can model the measured maximum change in peak diffraction intensity.

We focus next on the BTO (001) Bragg peak measured at longer time delays (Figure 1b). We observe that IXRD⁢(Eν)subscript𝐼XRDsubscript𝐸𝜈I_{\mathrm{XRD}}(E_{\nu})italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ν end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) at t>3 ps𝑡times3picosecondt>$3\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t > start_ARG 3 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG are shifted toward lower photon energies, i.e., larger out-of-plane lattice parameters c𝑐citalic_c, with respect to IXRD⁢(Eν)subscript𝐼XRDsubscript𝐸𝜈I_{\mathrm{XRD}}(E_{\nu})italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ν end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) at smaller delays t𝑡titalic_t. This can be clearly seen from the plot of the BTO out-of-plane strain η¯⁢(t)¯𝜂𝑡\overline{\eta}(t)over¯ start_ARG italic_η end_ARG ( italic_t ), averaged over dBTOsubscript𝑑BTOd_{\mathrm{BTO}}italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Figure 1d). Here, η¯⁢(t)=[c⁢(t)−c0]/c0¯𝜂𝑡delimited-[]𝑐𝑡subscript𝑐0subscript𝑐0\overline{\eta}(t)=[c(t)-c_{0}]/c_{0}over¯ start_ARG italic_η end_ARG ( italic_t ) = [ italic_c ( italic_t ) - italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ] / italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, with c⁢(t)𝑐𝑡c(t)italic_c ( italic_t ) and c0subscript𝑐0c_{0}italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT representing the average c¯¯𝑐\overline{c}over¯ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG at a given t>0 ps𝑡times0picosecondt>$0\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t > start_ARG 0 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG and t≤0 ps𝑡times0picosecondt\leq$0\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t ≤ start_ARG 0 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG, respectively (see Methods). In Figure 1d, we find that: (i) the maximum compression of −0.024%percent0.024-0.024\%- 0.024 % occurs at t=2.6 ps𝑡times2.6picosecondt=$2.6\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t = start_ARG 2.6 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG, (ii) η¯⁢(t)¯𝜂𝑡\overline{\eta}(t)over¯ start_ARG italic_η end_ARG ( italic_t ) increases linearly at a rate of 0.04 %/ps0.04\text{\,}\%\mathrm{/}\mathrm{p}\mathrm{s}start_ARG 0.04 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG % / roman_ps end_ARG in the range 4 ps<t<10 pstimes4picosecond𝑡times10picosecond$4\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$<t<$10\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$start_ARG 4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG < italic_t < start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG, and (iii) settles at 0.34%percent0.340.34\%0.34 % at ≈20 psabsenttimes20picosecond\approx$20\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$≈ start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG.

The model fitting η¯⁢(t)¯𝜂𝑡\overline{\eta}(t)over¯ start_ARG italic_η end_ARG ( italic_t ) data in Figure 1d is presented in the following. When a photon with energy E>Eg𝐸subscript𝐸𝑔E>E_{g}italic_E > italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is absorbed in BTO, electrons are photoexcited from the O 2p-derived valence band to the Ti 3d-derived conduction band [29, 30, 31]. The thermalization of photoexcited electrons leads to an increase in the electron temperature (Tesubscript𝑇𝑒T_{e}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT), and to changes in the electronic system that can be modeled by the variation of the bandgap as a function of the electronic pressure (∂Eg/∂psubscript𝐸𝑔𝑝\partial E_{g}/\partial p∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / ∂ italic_p) [4]. In turn, a modified electron system affects the interatomic potential, resulting in atomic motions and contributing to the deformation potential stress σD⁢P⁢(Te,∂Eg/∂p)subscript𝜎𝐷𝑃subscript𝑇𝑒subscript𝐸𝑔𝑝\sigma_{DP}(T_{e},\partial E_{g}/\partial p)italic_σ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_D italic_P end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , ∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / ∂ italic_p ). Subsequently, photoexcited electrons transfer part of their excess energy (E−Eg𝐸subscript𝐸𝑔E-E_{g}italic_E - italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) to the phonon system via electron-phonon scattering, increasing the phonon temperature (Tpsubscript𝑇𝑝T_{p}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) on the picosecond timescale. This, in turn, induces a lattice expansion dependent on the BTO linear expansion coefficient (β𝛽\betaitalic_β), and contributes to the thermoelastic stress σT⁢E⁢(Tp,β)subscript𝜎𝑇𝐸subscript𝑇𝑝𝛽\sigma_{TE}(T_{p},\beta)italic_σ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T italic_E end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_β ). The total stress[4, 32] σ=ρ⁢v2⁢η+σD⁢P+σT⁢E𝜎𝜌superscript𝑣2𝜂subscript𝜎𝐷𝑃subscript𝜎𝑇𝐸\sigma=\rho v^{2}\eta+\sigma_{DP}+\sigma_{TE}italic_σ = italic_ρ italic_v start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η + italic_σ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_D italic_P end_POSTSUBSCRIPT + italic_σ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T italic_E end_POSTSUBSCRIPT generates a strain wave η⁢(z,t)𝜂𝑧𝑡\eta(z,t)italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) that propagates through the material of mass density ρ𝜌\rhoitalic_ρ at the longitudinal speed of sound ν𝜈\nuitalic_ν. We solve analytically the two-temperature model (Supplementary Note 7) and the lattice strain wave equation (Supplementary Note 8) to obtain η⁢(z,t)𝜂𝑧𝑡\eta(z,t)italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ). Finally, we calculate the strain η¯⁢(t)¯𝜂𝑡\overline{\eta}(t)over¯ start_ARG italic_η end_ARG ( italic_t ), averaged over dBTOsubscript𝑑BTOd_{\mathrm{BTO}}italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, to fit the experimental data in Figure 1d. The main outcome of our fit model is a negative ∂Eg/∂psubscript𝐸𝑔𝑝\partial E_{g}/\partial p∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / ∂ italic_p of the order of ≈⁢10−31 J Pa−1absenttimesE-31timesjoulepascal1\approx${10}^{-31}\text{\,}\mathrm{J}\text{\,}{\mathrm{Pa}}^{-1}$≈ start_ARG start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG ⁢ end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG - 31 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_J end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_Pa end_ARG start_ARG - 1 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG, in agreement with first-principles calculations[33], with a resulting bandgap decrease of about 3.2 meVtimes3.2millielectronvolt3.2\text{\,}\mathrm{meV}start_ARG 3.2 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_meV end_ARG (Supplementary Note 10). The negative ∂Eg/∂psubscript𝐸𝑔𝑝\partial E_{g}/\partial p∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / ∂ italic_p causes lattice compression within the first ≈3 psabsenttimes3picosecond\approx$3\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$≈ start_ARG 3 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG, when σD⁢Psubscript𝜎𝐷𝑃\sigma_{DP}italic_σ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_D italic_P end_POSTSUBSCRIPT dominates over σT⁢Esubscript𝜎𝑇𝐸\sigma_{TE}italic_σ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T italic_E end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (inset of Figure 1d). Conversely, at larger time delays (t>3 ps𝑡times3picosecondt>$3\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t > start_ARG 3 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG), Tpsubscript𝑇𝑝T_{p}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT increases (Figure S17b) and the thermoelastic term becomes the dominant one, leading to lattice expansion (Figure S23).

Photoinduced ferroelectric polarization and electron dynamics

We turn now to investigating the dynamics of the ferroelectric polarization Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and of the photoexcited carriers [34, 35, 36, 37, 38], upon excitation of the BTO film by the same 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG pump laser with fluence 2.7 mJ cm−2times2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter22.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG. Therefore, we perform tr-SHG experiments [39, 27] and simultaneously tr-refl in reflection geometry (Figure 2a). From SHG polarimetry, i.e., the dependence of SHG intensity ISHG⁢(φ)∝|χi⁢j⁢k(2)⁢E⁢(ω)2|2proportional-tosubscript𝐼SHG𝜑superscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝑖𝑗𝑘𝐸superscript𝜔22I_{\mathrm{SHG}}(\varphi)\propto|\chi^{(2)}_{ijk}E(\omega)^{2}|^{2}italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_φ ) ∝ | italic_χ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i italic_j italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_E ( italic_ω ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT | start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT on the polarization angle of the probe beam φ𝜑\varphiitalic_φ, we learn about the optical tensor elements χi⁢j⁢k(2)subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝑖𝑗𝑘\chi^{(2)}_{ijk}italic_χ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i italic_j italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT of a material, and thus its symmetry[39]. By selecting either horizontal (p𝑝pitalic_p) or vertical (s𝑠sitalic_s) polarization of the SHG beam, we measure ISHGp⁢(φ)superscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑝𝜑I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{p}(\varphi)italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_φ ) and ISHGs⁢(φ)superscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑠𝜑I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{s}(\varphi)italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_φ ), shown in Figures 2b and c (blue points) together with the respective fit curves (see Methods), which are based on the 4mm point group symmetry with the following nonzero tensor elements: χz⁢x⁢x(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑥𝑥2\chi_{zxx}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_x italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, χx⁢x⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑥𝑥𝑧2\chi_{xxz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x italic_x italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, and χz⁢z⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑧𝑧2\chi_{zzz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_z italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT.

We observe a reduction of the BTO tetragonality, upon laser excitation, from the time evolution of χz⁢x⁢x(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑥𝑥2\chi_{zxx}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_x italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, χx⁢x⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑥𝑥𝑧2\chi_{xxz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x italic_x italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, and χz⁢z⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑧𝑧2\chi_{zzz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_z italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT in the delay range −1 ps<t<30 pstimes-1picosecond𝑡times30picosecond$-1\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$<t<$30\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$start_ARG - 1 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG < italic_t < start_ARG 30 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG. While the 4mm symmetry is preserved also after the pump excitation (orange points in Figures 2b-c), a laser-induced change in tetragonality is observed. To visualize it, we display the relative change Δ⁢χ/χ0Δ𝜒subscript𝜒0\Delta\chi/\chi_{0}roman_Δ italic_χ / italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT of χx⁢x⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑥𝑥𝑧2\chi_{xxz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x italic_x italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, χz⁢x⁢x(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑥𝑥2\chi_{zxx}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_x italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, and χz⁢z⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑧𝑧2\chi_{zzz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_z italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT as a function of delay t𝑡titalic_t (Figure 2d for −1 ps<t<3 pstimes-1picosecond𝑡times3picosecond$-1\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$<t<$3\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$start_ARG - 1 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG < italic_t < start_ARG 3 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG and Figure S13 for −1 ps<t<30 pstimes-1picosecond𝑡times30picosecond$-1\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$<t<$30\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$start_ARG - 1 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG < italic_t < start_ARG 30 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG). The three tensor elements show similar dynamics, characterized by a fast fall time with the maximum drop after ≈500 fsabsenttimes500femtosecond\approx$500\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$≈ start_ARG 500 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG and two exponential recovery time constants of ≈5.5 psabsenttimes5.5picosecond\approx$5.5\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$≈ start_ARG 5.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG and ≈45 psabsenttimes45picosecond\approx$45\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$≈ start_ARG 45 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG. Interestingly, the tensor elements representative of the electric dipole along the out-of-plane direction z𝑧zitalic_z (χz⁢x⁢x(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑥𝑥2\chi_{zxx}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_x italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and χz⁢z⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑧𝑧2\chi_{zzz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_z italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT) show a nearly identical time dependence and a larger relative change than χx⁢x⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑥𝑥𝑧2\chi_{xxz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x italic_x italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, which refers to the in-plane electric dipole along the direction x𝑥xitalic_x. The difference between χz⁢x⁢x(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑥𝑥2\chi_{zxx}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_x italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT (or χz⁢z⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑧𝑧2\chi_{zzz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_z italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT) and χx⁢x⁢z(2)superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑥𝑥𝑧2\chi_{xxz}^{(2)}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x italic_x italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT reaches 0.5%percent0.50.5\%0.5 % after ≈500 fsabsenttimes500femtosecond\approx$500\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$≈ start_ARG 500 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG and decreases in a few tens of picoseconds (Figure S13). A purely thermal effect [40] would cause a uniform change of all tensor elements χi⁢j⁢k(2)subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝑖𝑗𝑘\chi^{(2)}_{ijk}italic_χ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i italic_j italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, whereas the measured different dynamics of χi⁢j⁢k(2)subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝑖𝑗𝑘\chi^{(2)}_{ijk}italic_χ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i italic_j italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT indicates a time-dependent lattice distortion of non-thermal origin. In fact, TD-DFT calculations[29, 30] show that upon charge transfer, the Ti-O∥subscriptOparallel-to\mathrm{O_{\parallel}}roman_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∥ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT bonds between Ti and apical O∥subscriptOparallel-to\mathrm{O_{\parallel}}roman_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∥ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT atoms (parallel to Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) are weakened more than Ti-O⟂subscriptOperpendicular-to\mathrm{O_{\perp}}roman_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⟂ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT bonds between Ti and basal O⟂subscriptOperpendicular-to\mathrm{O_{\perp}}roman_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⟂ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT atoms (perpendicular to Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT), with a resulting reduction of the tetragonal distortion (inset of Figure 2d). Consequently, it is intuitive to expect a larger amplitude of the induced electric dipole along the Ti-O∥subscriptOparallel-to\mathrm{O_{\parallel}}roman_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∥ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT direction (z𝑧zitalic_z) with respect to the Ti-O⟂subscriptOperpendicular-to\mathrm{O_{\perp}}roman_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⟂ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT direction (in the x⁢y𝑥𝑦xyitalic_x italic_y plane), as experimentally demonstrated by our data.

The proportionality ISHG∝|χi⁢j⁢k(2)|2∝|Ps|2proportional-tosubscript𝐼SHGsuperscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝑖𝑗𝑘2proportional-tosuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝑠2I_{\mathrm{SHG}}\propto|\chi^{(2)}_{ijk}|^{2}\propto|P_{s}|^{2}italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∝ | italic_χ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i italic_j italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT | start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∝ | italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT | start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT gives direct access to the magnitude of the spontaneous polarization [39]. To this end, we measure the relative change Δ⁢ISHGp/ISHG,0pΔsuperscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑝superscriptsubscript𝐼SHG0𝑝\Delta I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{p}/I_{\mathrm{SHG,0}}^{p}roman_Δ italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT / italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT as a function of pump-probe delay t𝑡titalic_t (Figure 2e), with the polarization of the 800 nmtimes800nanometer800\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 800 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG probe beam fixed to the maximum of ISHGp⁢(φ)superscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑝𝜑I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{p}(\varphi)italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_φ ) at φ=0⁢°𝜑0°\varphi=0\degreeitalic_φ = 0 ° (Figure 2b). Simultaneously, we measure the relative change in reflectivity Δ⁢R/R0Δ𝑅subscript𝑅0\Delta R/R_{0}roman_Δ italic_R / italic_R start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT as a function of pump-probe delay t𝑡titalic_t (Figure 2f). The data in Figure 2e [f] are well reproduced by a fit function consisting of the sum of three exponential decay terms, with fall [rise] time τ0subscript𝜏0\tau_{0}italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and recovery times τ1subscript𝜏1\tau_{1}italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and τ2subscript𝜏2\tau_{2}italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, convoluted with a Gaussian function representing the experimental temporal resolution (Supplementary Note 2). The initial drop in SHG intensity by 10%percent1010\%10 % within 350 fstimes350femtosecond350\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}start_ARG 350 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG is followed by τ1SHG=7.2⁢(5) pssuperscriptsubscript𝜏1SHGtimesuncertain7.25picosecond\tau_{1}^{\mathrm{SHG}}=$7.2(5)\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = start_ARG start_ARG 7.2 end_ARG start_ARG ( 5 ) end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG and τ2SHG=200⁢(140) pssuperscriptsubscript𝜏2SHGtimesuncertain200140picosecond\tau_{2}^{\mathrm{SHG}}=$200(140)\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = start_ARG start_ARG 200 end_ARG start_ARG ( 140 ) end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG recovery times, resulting in a 2.3%percent2.32.3\%2.3 % drop at 40 pstimes40picosecond40\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}start_ARG 40 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG (Figure 2e). At the same time, we observe a fast increase in reflectivity by 7%percent77\%7 % within 350 fstimes350femtosecond350\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}start_ARG 350 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG, followed by two recovery times, τ1R=5.2⁢(1) pssuperscriptsubscript𝜏1𝑅timesuncertain5.21picosecond\tau_{1}^{R}=$5.2(1)\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_R end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = start_ARG start_ARG 5.2 end_ARG start_ARG ( 1 ) end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG and τ2R=29.8⁢(5) pssuperscriptsubscript𝜏2𝑅timesuncertain29.85picosecond\tau_{2}^{R}=$29.8(5)\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_R end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = start_ARG start_ARG 29.8 end_ARG start_ARG ( 5 ) end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG (Figure 2f).

In both tr-SHG and tr-refl data, the time needed to reach the maximum relative change (350 fstimes350femtosecond350\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}start_ARG 350 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG) might be due to the thermalization of photoexcited electrons via electron-electron scattering. Subsequently, thermalized electrons, which are higher in the conduction band, move to the bottom of the conduction band, transferring energy to the phonon system, and recombining with holes in the valence band via electron-phonon scattering[41, 4, 37] or radiatively[42]. These processes are characterized by the recovery times τ1subscript𝜏1\tau_{1}italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and τ2subscript𝜏2\tau_{2}italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Both τ1subscript𝜏1\tau_{1}italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and τ2subscript𝜏2\tau_{2}italic_τ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT recovery constants of Δ⁢ISHGp/ISHG,0pΔsubscriptsuperscript𝐼𝑝SHGsubscriptsuperscript𝐼𝑝SHG0\Delta I^{p}_{\mathrm{SHG}}/I^{p}_{\mathrm{SHG,0}}roman_Δ italic_I start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / italic_I start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are larger than those of Δ⁢R/R0Δ𝑅subscript𝑅0\Delta R/R_{0}roman_Δ italic_R / italic_R start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT because the dynamics of the spontaneous polarization results from the convolution of the faster dynamics of photoexcited carriers (seen by tr-refl) and the slower dynamics of atoms.

To interpret the SHG intensity drop and the reflectivity increase, it is useful to express the spontaneous polarization as[22, 43]: Ps⁢(t)=1/V⁢∑iqi⁢(t)⁢Δ⁢zi⁢(t)subscript𝑃𝑠𝑡1𝑉subscript𝑖subscript𝑞𝑖𝑡Δsubscript𝑧𝑖𝑡P_{s}(t)=1/V\sum_{i}q_{i}(t)\Delta z_{i}(t)italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_t ) = 1 / italic_V ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_t ) roman_Δ italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_t ), where V𝑉Vitalic_V is the volume of the unit cell, qi⁢(t)subscript𝑞𝑖𝑡q_{i}(t)italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_t ) is the Born effective charge and Δ⁢zi⁢(t)Δsubscript𝑧𝑖𝑡\Delta z_{i}(t)roman_Δ italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_t ) is the out-of-plane displacement of atom i𝑖iitalic_i. The above-bandgap photoexcitation transfers electrons from the O 2p-derived valence band to the Ti 3d-derived conduction band of BTO. This charge transfer from O to Ti atoms reduces the corresponding charges qisubscript𝑞𝑖q_{i}italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. We attribute changes in R𝑅Ritalic_R to changes in photoexcited carrier density nesubscript𝑛𝑒n_{e}italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT[35, 36, 37], thus contributing to qisubscript𝑞𝑖q_{i}italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, while Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT results from changes in both qisubscript𝑞𝑖q_{i}italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and Δ⁢ziΔsubscript𝑧𝑖\Delta z_{i}roman_Δ italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. After 350 fstimes350femtosecond350\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}start_ARG 350 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG, before atomic movements can occur, the increase in carrier density nesubscript𝑛𝑒n_{e}italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is responsible for the measured decrease in Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and increase in R𝑅Ritalic_R, shown in Figures 2e-f. This interpretation is corroborated by the increase of the maximum relative change of both Δ⁢ISHGp/ISHG,0pΔsubscriptsuperscript𝐼𝑝SHGsubscriptsuperscript𝐼𝑝SHG0\Delta I^{p}_{\mathrm{SHG}}/I^{p}_{\mathrm{SHG,0}}roman_Δ italic_I start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / italic_I start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and Δ⁢R/R0Δ𝑅subscript𝑅0\Delta R/R_{0}roman_Δ italic_R / italic_R start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT with pump fluence (Figure S14 and Figure S15).

Discussion

We present here a unified picture in five stages of the lattice, polarization, and electron dynamics data presented above. Our observations are summarized in Figures 3a-e, and the physical mechanisms involved are sketched in Figures 3f-h. Before the arrival of the pump laser, the BTO is characterized by an out-of-plane polarization with given Born effective charges at each atomic site (stage 1, Figure 3a). Upon absorption of the pump laser at t=0 ps𝑡times0picosecondt=$0\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t = start_ARG 0 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG, electrons move from the occupied O-derived valence band to the unoccupied Ti-derived conduction band (Figure 3f). Within ≈350 fsabsenttimes350femtosecond\approx$350\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$≈ start_ARG 350 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG we observe the maximum increase in the photoexcited carrier density nesubscript𝑛𝑒n_{e}italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, as indicated by the increase in Δ⁢R/R0Δ𝑅subscript𝑅0\Delta R/R_{0}roman_Δ italic_R / italic_R start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. This charge transfer reduces the Born effective charges qisubscript𝑞𝑖q_{i}italic_q start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT at Ti and O atoms, thereby decreasing the spontaneous polarization Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, as indicated by the decrease in Δ⁢ISHGp/ISHG,0pΔsuperscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑝superscriptsubscript𝐼SHG0𝑝\Delta I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{p}/I_{\mathrm{SHG,0}}^{p}roman_Δ italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT / italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT (stage 2, Figure 3b). A smaller Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is also consistent with a larger screening of long-range Coulomb interactions, which favor off-center atomic displacements and thus are responsible for the polar order. This effect is modeled by DFT calculations[44, 45], showing that an increase in photoexcited carriers in the conduction band of BTO indeed tends to induce a phase transition from the ferroelectric to the paraelectric phase. Moreover, TD-DFT calculations[29, 30] predict that such photoinduced change of BTO electronic structure weakens more significantly Ti-O∥ bonds, parallel to Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, as compared to Ti-O⟂ bonds, perpendicular to Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. We demonstrate this effect experimentally by measuring a larger change of the out-of-plane tensor elements (χz⁢x⁢xsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑥𝑥\chi_{zxx}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_x italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, χz⁢z⁢zsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑧𝑧\chi_{zzz}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_z italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) as compared to the in-plane tensor element (χx⁢x⁢zsubscript𝜒𝑥𝑥𝑧\chi_{xxz}italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x italic_x italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT). In stage 2, in contrast to the maximum change in carrier density nesubscript𝑛𝑒n_{e}italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and polarization Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, the lattice remains unperturbed: this marks the onset of the decoupling between polarization and strain. At these early time delays, the bulk photovoltaic effect (BPVE) [12] and the Schottky interface effect [11] could potentially play a role, but we explain in the following why they are not dominant in our experiments.

First, in photoexcited ferroelectric materials it is common to observe the BPVE, i.e., the generation of photovoltage under light illumination [46, 47, 48]. The BPVE occurs under two conditions: the presence of a noncentrosymmetrical crystal and the excitation of nonthermalized electrons [46]. In our system both conditions are satisfied shortly after t=0 ps𝑡times0picosecondt=$0\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t = start_ARG 0 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG, i.e., before hot electron thermalization takes place (Figure 3f). In BTO, if the light polarization is perpendicular to the spontaneous polarization Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, the induced photovoltage is parallel to Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [49, 50]. This enhances Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and induces, via the inverse piezoelectric effect, lattice expansion, similarly to what was observed in BiFeO3 [51]. However, in our study, although the polarization of the pump laser is perpendicular to Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Figure 2a), within ≈350 fsabsenttimes350femtosecond\approx$350\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$≈ start_ARG 350 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG, when electrons are non-thermal and BFVE could potentially play a role, we see a decrease in Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Figure 2e). Hence, we conclude that BPVE is not a dominant effect in our experiments. In fact, this observation can be explained by the wavelength dependence of the BPVE, which shows a maximum when the photon energy is close to Egsubscript𝐸𝑔E_{g}italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, as observed, e.g., in Refs. [12, 52]. In contrast, in BTO at λ=266 nm𝜆times266nanometer\lambda=$266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$italic_λ = start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG the contribution of BVPE is negligible, as observed in experiments [49, 50] and calculations [47].

Second, ferroelectric thin films grown on a metal substrate form a Schottky interface, where the electron-hole pairs generated by light absorption are separated by the built-in voltage of the Schottky barrier and cause a power-independent enhancement of the polarization for Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT pointing up (toward the surface) [11]. This effect can be excluded as responsible for our experimental observations because: (i) although our sample has Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT pointing up (Figure S3), we see a decrease in Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and (ii) the measured change in Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT linearly depends on the incident fluence (Figure S14). In fact, the Schottky interface effect is expected to play a significant role for films of thickness ≈\approx≈ 10 nmtimes10nanometer10\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG or thinner [11], and to be negligible in our case due to the three-fold greater film thickness.

In the 350 fs−3.5 pstimes350femtosecondtimes3.5picosecond$350\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$-$3.5\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$start_ARG 350 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG - start_ARG 3.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG delay range (stage 3, Figure 3c), we observe polarization and strain following opposite trends. In fact, while the displacement between Ti and the center of the O octahedron as well as the strain decreases, Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT starts to increase. This is attributed to the initial recombination of photoexcited carriers, suggested by the incipient recovery in Δ⁢R/RΔ𝑅𝑅\Delta R/Rroman_Δ italic_R / italic_R. At this stage, we also observe lattice compression caused by the negative parameter ∂Eg/∂psubscript𝐸𝑔𝑝\partial E_{g}/\partial p∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / ∂ italic_p via the deformation potential. Our observation is in line with theory [53, 45, 30] predicting a reduced bandgap Egsubscript𝐸𝑔E_{g}italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT due to the presence of photoinduced carrier density (Figure 3g). Moreover, lattice contraction via the inverse piezoelectric effect is also consistent with an overall reduced Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT caused by photoexcited carriers [54].

In the 3.5−73.573.5-73.5 - 7  pstimesabsentpicosecond\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG delay range (stage 4, Figure 3d), the relative displacement between Ti and O atoms tends to restore the ground state with a larger Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. At the same time, the relaxation of photocarriers to the top of the conduction band and their recombination result in a further increase of the Born effective charges, and an energy transfer to the phonon temperature (Figure 3g), which, in turn, leads to lattice expansion. The latter effects persist up to t≈20 ps𝑡times20picosecondt\approx$20\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t ≈ start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG.

At t≈20 ps𝑡times20picosecondt\approx$20\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t ≈ start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG (stage 5, Figure 3e), together with a further relaxation of the electronic system toward equilibrium, we observe a saturation of the BTO average strain. This results in a metastable state with a slightly reduced Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and a significantly increased out-of-plane strain with respect to the ground state. The reduced Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is attributed to the presence of residual photoexcited carriers in the conduction band (Figure 3h), while the increased out-of-plane strain is due to the thermoelastic contribution. We exclude the depolarization field screening as the main driving effect for the transient tensile strain seen in our experiments, because it would lead to the typical saturation of the strain with increasing pump fluence [55]. For example, in PbTiO3 [12] the metastable lattice strain reaches saturation with 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG laser fluences of 0.01 mJ cm−2times0.01timesmillijoulecentimeter20.01\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 0.01 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG. Conversely, in our study, we employ two orders of magnitude larger pump fluence and still the metastable strain scales approximately linearly with the pump fluence (Figure 1d and Figure S18). In addition, on the ≈20 psabsenttimes20picosecond\approx$20\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$≈ start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG timescale, the deformation potential plays a minor role (Figure S23), thus its influence on the bandgap is expected to be marginal. Interestingly, although the out-of-plane lattice parameter is larger than in the ground state (c0subscript𝑐0c_{0}italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT), the polarization Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is still smaller compared to equilibrium. This underscores the persistence of the decoupling between polarization and strain. Quantitatively, we estimate that in stage 5 the contribution of photoexcited electrons to Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT has ≈10%absentpercent10\approx 10\%≈ 10 % larger magnitude than the structural contribution from the strain η𝜂\etaitalic_η (Supplementary Note 11).

In summary, we demonstrate that, with an above-bandgap laser excitation, an ultrafast decoupling of spontaneous polarization and strain can be achieved and measured. We assign this effect to the dominant contribution of photoexcited electrons in determining the spontaneous polarization when the system is out of equilibrium, and show that for an accurate description and fundamental understanding of a ferroelectric material in a photoexcited state, it is essential to combine multiple techniques that address the dynamical evolution of the various degrees of freedom, i.e., lattice, ferroelectric polarization, and electrons. The decoupling of polarization from the strain offers the opportunity to change paradigm from strain engineering [56] to light-induced polarization engineering, thereby lifting the constraint of selecting among a limited number of substrates [24] or designing freestanding membranes [26] to tune the spontaneous polarization. Moreover, by softening the Ti-O∥ bonds, we bring BTO to an excited state, where it could be further modified by  THztimesabsentterahertz\text{\,}\mathrm{THz}start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_THz end_ARG light to achieve stable and reversible polarization switching at lower fluences than otherwise needed when starting from the ground state [21]. Finally, the transient and reversible control of the spontaneous polarization, shown in this study, offers a pathway to control by light both electric and magnetic degrees of freedom in multiferroic materials.

Methods

Sample preparation and characterization

The epitaxial bilayers BTO/SRO were grown on a GSO substrate using pulsed laser deposition. The ceramic targets of SRO and BTO were 8 cmtimes8centimeter8\text{\,}\mathrm{cm}start_ARG 8 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG away from the substrate and ablated using a KrF excimer laser (λ=248 nm𝜆times248nanometer\lambda=$248\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$italic_λ = start_ARG 248 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG, fluence 5.4 J cm−2times5.4timesjoulecentimeter25.4\text{\,}\mathrm{J}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 5.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_J end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG, 2 Hztimes2hertz2\text{\,}\mathrm{Hz}start_ARG 2 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_Hz end_ARG repetition rate). The deposition of SRO and BTO layers was conducted in O2 atmosphere with pressure pO2 = 100 mTorr and deposition temperatures of 908 Ktimes908kelvin908\text{\,}\mathrm{K}start_ARG 908 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_K end_ARG and 973 Ktimes973kelvin973\text{\,}\mathrm{K}start_ARG 973 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_K end_ARG, respectively. Sample cooling with the rate of 3 K min−1times3timeskelvinminute13\text{\,}\mathrm{K}\text{\,}{\mathrm{min}}^{-1}start_ARG 3 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_K end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_min end_ARG start_ARG - 1 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG was conducted in the environment of saturated O2 (pO2 = 104superscript10410^{4}10 start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 4 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT mTorr) to prevent the formation of oxygen vacancies. The thicknesses of BTO and SRO layers, dBTO=34.5 nmsubscript𝑑BTOtimes34.5nanometerd_{\mathrm{BTO}}=$34.5\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 34.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG and dSRO=47 nmsubscript𝑑SROtimes47nanometerd_{\mathrm{SRO}}=$47\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SRO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 47 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG are extracted from a θ𝜃\thetaitalic_θ-2⁢θ2𝜃2\theta2 italic_θ scan of the as-grown sample around the (002) reflections (Figure S1), while the GSO substrate is 0.5 mmtimes0.5millimeter0.5\text{\,}\mathrm{mm}start_ARG 0.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_mm end_ARG thick. We determine the out-of-plane lattice parameters cBTO=4.074 Åsubscript𝑐BTOtimes4.074angstromc_{\mathrm{BTO}}=$4.074\text{\,}\mathrm{\text{\r{A}}}$italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 4.074 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG angstrom end_ARG, cSRO=3.934 Åsubscript𝑐SROtimes3.934angstromc_{\mathrm{SRO}}=$3.934\text{\,}\mathrm{\text{\r{A}}}$italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SRO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 3.934 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG angstrom end_ARG, and cGSO=3.964 Åsubscript𝑐GSOtimes3.964angstromc_{\mathrm{GSO}}=$3.964\text{\,}\mathrm{\text{\r{A}}}$italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_GSO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 3.964 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG angstrom end_ARG by means of the reciprocal space map shown in Figure S2. The in-plane lattice parameter a=3.970 Å𝑎times3.970angstroma=$3.970\text{\,}\mathrm{\text{\r{A}}}$italic_a = start_ARG 3.970 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG angstrom end_ARG, common to BTO, SRO and GSO, indicates that both BTO and SRO thin films are coherently strained to the substrate. Furthermore, the absence of satellite peaks in Figure S2 suggests the existence of a BTO monodomain. We determine that the spontaneous polarization of the as-grown sample Pssubscript𝑃𝑠P_{s}italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT points upward (toward the surface) by means of piezoresponse force microscopy (Figure S3). The linear expansion coefficients of BTO above Tc=400 °Csubscript𝑇𝑐times400celsiusT_{c}=$400\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolCelsius}$italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 400 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG °C end_ARG, of SRO and GSO in the temperature range 35 °C<T<700 °Ctimes35celsius𝑇times700celsius$35\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolCelsius}$<T<$700\text{\,}\mathrm{% \SIUnitSymbolCelsius}$start_ARG 35 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG °C end_ARG < italic_T < start_ARG 700 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG °C end_ARG are determined by θ𝜃\thetaitalic_θ-2⁢θ2𝜃2\theta2 italic_θ scans as a function of sample temperature (Figure S4).

Time-resolved X-ray diffraction

Time-resolved X-ray diffraction experiments were performed at the Spectroscopy and Coherent Scattering instrument (SCS) of the European X-Ray Free-Electron Laser Facility (EuXFEL), using an optical laser (OL) as pump and the XFEL as probe. The XFEL pulse pattern consisted of pulse trains at a repetition rate of 10 Hztimes10hertz10\text{\,}\mathrm{Hz}start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_Hz end_ARG, with 35353535 pulses per train at the intratrain repetition rate of 113 kHztimes113kilohertz113\text{\,}\mathrm{kHz}start_ARG 113 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_kHz end_ARG. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the XFEL spectrum was 11.7 eVtimes11.7electronvolt11.7\text{\,}\mathrm{eV}start_ARG 11.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_eV end_ARG. To reduce the energy bandwidth, the XFEL beam was monochromatized using a variable line spacing grating with 50505050 lines/mm in the first diffraction order and exit slits with a gap of 100 µ⁢mtimes100micrometer100\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolMicro m}start_ARG 100 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG, providing an energy resolution of ≈650 meVabsenttimes650millielectronvolt\approx$650\text{\,}\mathrm{meV}$≈ start_ARG 650 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_meV end_ARG. The nominal pulse duration of the XFEL pulses was ≈25 fsabsenttimes25femtosecond\approx$25\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$≈ start_ARG 25 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG, with pulse stretching at the monochromator grating of ≈10 fsabsenttimes10femtosecond\approx$10\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$≈ start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG (FWHM). The XFEL pulses, with initial energy of 1.5 mJtimes1.5millijoule1.5\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}start_ARG 1.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG per pulse, were then attenuated by transmission through a gas attenuator (GATT), consisting of a volume containing N2subscriptN2\mathrm{N_{2}}roman_N start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT gas at a variable pressure. In order to prevent detector saturation, the transmission of the GATT was set to have ≈15 nJabsenttimes15nanojoule\approx$15\text{\,}\mathrm{nJ}$≈ start_ARG 15 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nJ end_ARG per pulse at the sample. The XFEL pulse energy was measured by an X-ray gas monitor detector (XGM), located ≈7 mabsenttimes7meter\approx$7\text{\,}\mathrm{m}$≈ start_ARG 7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_m end_ARG upstream of the sample, just before the Kirkpatrick-Baez (KB) mirrors. The latter were used to focus the XFEL beam at the sample to a spot size of wxXFEL×wyXFEL=140 µ⁢m×100 µ⁢msubscriptsuperscript𝑤XFEL𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝑤XFEL𝑦times140micrometertimes100micrometerw^{\mathrm{XFEL}}_{x}\times w^{\mathrm{XFEL}}_{y}=$140\text{\,}\mathrm{% \SIUnitSymbolMicro m}$\times$100\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolMicro m}$italic_w start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_XFEL end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT × italic_w start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_XFEL end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_y end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 140 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG × start_ARG 100 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG (determined by knife edge scans), where w𝑤witalic_w is defined as the distance from the beam axis where the intensity drops to 1/e21superscript𝑒21/e^{2}1 / italic_e start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT of the value on the beam axis. The angle of incidence (defined from the sample surface) of XFEL and OL beams at the sample was 86 °times86degree86\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}start_ARG 86 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG ° end_ARG and 85 °times85degree85\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}start_ARG 85 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG ° end_ARG, respectively. The intensity of each XFEL pulse diffracted by the sample was measured by a Si avalanche photodiode (APD, model SAR3000G1X, Laser Components), converted to a voltage pulse and digitized. To prevent the pump laser intensity from reaching the APD, the latter was equipped with a filter made of 400 nmtimes400nanometer400\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 400 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG Ti, deposited on 200 nmtimes200nanometer200\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 200 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG polyimide.

The pump laser had a central wavelength of 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG, the same pulse pattern as the XFEL with pulse duration of 70 fstimes70femtosecond70\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}start_ARG 70 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG, and beam size wxOL×wyOL=330 µ⁢m×240 µ⁢msubscriptsuperscript𝑤OL𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝑤OL𝑦times330micrometertimes240micrometerw^{\mathrm{OL}}_{x}\times w^{\mathrm{OL}}_{y}=$330\text{\,}\mathrm{% \SIUnitSymbolMicro m}$\times$240\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolMicro m}$italic_w start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_OL end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT × italic_w start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_OL end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_y end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 330 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG × start_ARG 240 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG, determined by knife edge scans. The transmittance profile of the 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG laser in our BTO/SRO/GSO sample is reported in Supplementary Note 6.1. The incident pump laser fluence at the sample was 2.7 mJ cm−2times2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter22.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG, and we verified that diffraction curves IXRD⁢(Eν)subscript𝐼XRDsubscript𝐸𝜈I_{\mathrm{XRD}}(E_{\nu})italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ν end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) measured at negative delays t𝑡titalic_t coincide with those measured without the pump laser (Figure S7). This confirms the reversibility of the pump effect induced by UV laser light. The temporal overlap between XFEL and OL was determined as detailed in Figure S8. The diffracted intensity of the XFEL pulses in a train was averaged and the corresponding time delay of the OL was corrected by the respective bunch arrival monitor (BAM) value, obtaining a time resolution Δ⁢t≈90 fsΔ𝑡times90femtosecond\Delta t\approx$90\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$roman_Δ italic_t ≈ start_ARG 90 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG (Supplementary Note 4). Data measured with incident pump laser fluence of 1.4 mJ cm−2times1.4timesmillijoulecentimeter21.4\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 1.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG are reported in Supplementary Note 8 and Supplementary Note 9.

In general, two kinds of tr-XRD experiments were performed: photon energy scans at a fixed pump-probe delays t𝑡titalic_t (Figure 1b and Figure S5), and time delay scans at a fixed photon energy Eν=1525 eVsubscript𝐸𝜈times1525electronvoltE_{\nu}=$1525\text{\,}\mathrm{eV}$italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ν end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 1525 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_eV end_ARG (Figure 1c and Figure S8). The energy scans were carried out by a simultaneous movement of the monochromator grating and the undulators gap, such to have always the peak of the XFEL spectrum at the desired photon energy. From the energy scans at different t𝑡titalic_t, the out-of-plane lattice parameters c⁢(t)𝑐𝑡c(t)italic_c ( italic_t ) and c0subscript𝑐0c_{0}italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are calculated as the center-of-mass of the BTO diffraction intensity IXRD⁢(t)subscript𝐼XRD𝑡I_{\mathrm{XRD}}(t)italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_t ) and refer to the average c𝑐citalic_c over dBTOsubscript𝑑BTOd_{\mathrm{BTO}}italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (c¯¯𝑐\overline{c}over¯ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG). Specifically, the BTO average out-of-plane parameters c¯¯𝑐\overline{c}over¯ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG is calculated from the corresponding (001) Bragg peaks using the Bragg condition c¯=(12 400 eV Å)/(2⁢E¯ν⁢sin⁡θ)¯𝑐times12400timeselectronvoltangstrom2subscript¯𝐸𝜈𝜃\overline{c}=($12\,400\text{\,}\mathrm{eV}\text{\,}\mathrm{\text{\r{A}}}$)/(2% \overline{E}_{\nu}\sin\theta)over¯ start_ARG italic_c end_ARG = ( start_ARG 12 400 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_eV end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG angstrom end_ARG end_ARG ) / ( 2 over¯ start_ARG italic_E end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ν end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_sin italic_θ ), where E¯νsubscript¯𝐸𝜈\overline{E}_{\nu}over¯ start_ARG italic_E end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ν end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the average of energy values, around the (001) BTO peak, weighted by IXRD⁢(Eν)subscript𝐼XRDsubscript𝐸𝜈I_{\mathrm{XRD}}(E_{\nu})italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ν end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ). Energy scans at different pump-probe delays t𝑡titalic_t over the photon energy range covering also GSO (001) and SRO (001) Bragg peaks are reported in Figure S9.

Time-resolved SHG and reflectivity

Time-resolved Second Harmonic Generation and time-resolved reflectivity experiments were performed at the SCS instrument of the EuXFEL using the same optical laser employed for tr-XRD experiments and the same pulse pattern. A sketch of the setup is shown in Figure 2a. The 800 nmtimes800nanometer800\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 800 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG probe beam at frequency ω𝜔\omegaitalic_ω (red arrow) impinges on the sample at angle θ=50 °𝜃times50degree\theta=$50\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}$italic_θ = start_ARG 50 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG ° end_ARG (defined from the normal to the surface) with polarization defined by the angle φ𝜑\varphiitalic_φ and varied by rotating a half waveplate. The angle φ=0 °𝜑times0degree\varphi=$0\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}$italic_φ = start_ARG 0 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG ° end_ARG [φ=90 °𝜑times90degree\varphi=$90\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolDegree}$italic_φ = start_ARG 90 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG ° end_ARG] refers to p𝑝pitalic_p [s𝑠sitalic_s] polarized light. The 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG pump beam at frequency 3⁢ω3𝜔3\omega3 italic_ω (purple arrow) impinges on the sample at normal incidence with p𝑝pitalic_p polarization. The 800 nmtimes800nanometer800\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 800 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG probe beam is then reflected by the sample and a dichroic mirror before reaching a Si photodiode. The latter is used to measure tr-refl of our sample. The 400 nmtimes400nanometer400\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 400 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG beam at frequency 2⁢ω2𝜔2\omega2 italic_ω (blue arrow) is the SHG signal generated in the BTO sample (Figure S11). This SHG beam is transmitted through the dichroic mirror and Glan polarizer, which is set to select either the p𝑝pitalic_p or s𝑠sitalic_s component of the electric field, corresponding to the p𝑝pitalic_p-out or s𝑠sitalic_s-out configuration, respectively. Finally, the SHG beam is filtered by a 400 nmtimes400nanometer400\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 400 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG bandpass filter before reaching a photomultiplier (model H10721-210-Y004, Hamamatsu). The azimuthal rotation of the sample around the z𝑧zitalic_z axis is defined by the angle γ𝛾\gammaitalic_γ. The independence of both polar plots of ISHGp⁢(φ)superscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑝𝜑I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{p}(\varphi)italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_φ ) and ISHGs⁢(φ)superscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑠𝜑I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{s}(\varphi)italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_φ ) from γ𝛾\gammaitalic_γ confirms the out-of-plane nature of the spontaneous polarization of our BTO sample (Figure S12). Both Si photodiode and photomultiplier measure respectively the reflectivity and SHG signal of each OL pulse in the train. The pulse duration of the 800 nmtimes800nanometer800\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 800 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG probe and 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG pump beams were approximately 50 fstimes50femtosecond50\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}start_ARG 50 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG and 70 fstimes70fs70\text{\,}\mathrm{f}\mathrm{s}start_ARG 70 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG, providing a time resolution of ≈90 fsabsenttimes90femtosecond\approx$90\text{\,}\mathrm{fs}$≈ start_ARG 90 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_fs end_ARG. The beam sizes of 800 nmtimes800nanometer800\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 800 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG and 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG beams, determined by knife edge scans, were wx800 nm×wy800 nm=55 µ⁢m×46 µ⁢msubscriptsuperscript𝑤times800nanometer𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝑤times800nanometer𝑦times55micrometertimes46micrometerw^{$800\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$}_{x}\times w^{$800\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$}_{y}=$55% \text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolMicro m}$\times$46\text{\,}\mathrm{% \SIUnitSymbolMicro m}$italic_w start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_ARG 800 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT × italic_w start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_ARG 800 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_y end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 55 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG × start_ARG 46 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG and wx266 nm×wy266 nm=165 µ⁢m×311 µ⁢msubscriptsuperscript𝑤times266nanometer𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝑤times266nanometer𝑦times165micrometertimes311micrometerw^{$266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$}_{x}\times w^{$266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$}_{y}=$165% \text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolMicro m}$\times$311\text{\,}\mathrm{% \SIUnitSymbolMicro m}$italic_w start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT × italic_w start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_y end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 165 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG × start_ARG 311 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_m end_ARG, respectively. The penetration depths of 800 nmtimes800nanometer800\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 800 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG, 400 nmtimes400nanometer400\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 400 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG and 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG laser beams in BTO are reported in Supplementary Note 6.2. While the fluence of the 800 nmtimes800nanometer800\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 800 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG probe beam was kept at 1.3 mJ cm−2times1.3timesmillijoulecentimeter21.3\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 1.3 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG, the fluence of the 266 nmtimes266nanometer266\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}start_ARG 266 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG pump beam was set to 2.7 mJ cm−2times2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter22.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG for the data displayed in Figure 2. tr-SHG and tr-refl data with fluences between 1.4 mJ cm−2times1.4timesmillijoulecentimeter21.4\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 1.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG and 12.3 mJ cm−2times12.3timesmillijoulecentimeter212.3\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 12.3 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG are reported in Figure S14 and Figure S15.

In general, given the incoming electric field with components Ej⁢(ω)subscript𝐸𝑗𝜔E_{j}(\omega)italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_ω ) and Ek⁢(ω)subscript𝐸𝑘𝜔E_{k}(\omega)italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_ω ) at frequency ω𝜔\omegaitalic_ω along j𝑗jitalic_j and k𝑘kitalic_k directions, the electric dipole polarization induced in the material at frequency 2⁢ω2𝜔2\omega2 italic_ω along the direction i𝑖iitalic_i is Pi⁢(2⁢ω)=χi⁢j⁢k(2)⁢Ej⁢(ω)⁢Ek⁢(ω)subscript𝑃𝑖2𝜔subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝑖𝑗𝑘subscript𝐸𝑗𝜔subscript𝐸𝑘𝜔P_{i}(2\omega)=\chi^{(2)}_{ijk}E_{j}(\omega)E_{k}(\omega)italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( 2 italic_ω ) = italic_χ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i italic_j italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_ω ) italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_ω ), where χi⁢j⁢k(2)subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝑖𝑗𝑘\chi^{(2)}_{ijk}italic_χ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i italic_j italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the second-order susceptibility, i.e., a third rank tensor reflecting the symmetry of the material. Each direction i𝑖iitalic_i, j𝑗jitalic_j, k𝑘kitalic_k can be x𝑥xitalic_x, or y𝑦yitalic_y, or z𝑧zitalic_z directions (Figure 2a). By selecting p𝑝pitalic_p or s𝑠sitalic_s polarization of the SHG beam, we measure ISHGp⁢(φ)superscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑝𝜑I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{p}(\varphi)italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_φ ) or ISHGs⁢(φ)superscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑠𝜑I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{s}(\varphi)italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_φ ), which, for a BTO single crystal with 4mm point group symmetry, can be expressed as[39, 11]:

ISHGp⁢(φ)∝(χz⁢x⁢x⁢sin⁡θ⁢cos⁡φ2+(2⁢χx⁢x⁢z⁢cot⁡θ2+χz⁢x⁢x⁢cot⁡θ2+χz⁢z⁢z)⁢sin⁡θ3⁢sin⁡φ2)2,proportional-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑝𝜑superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑥𝑥𝜃superscript𝜑22subscript𝜒𝑥𝑥𝑧superscript𝜃2subscript𝜒𝑧𝑥𝑥superscript𝜃2subscript𝜒𝑧𝑧𝑧superscript𝜃3superscript𝜑22I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{p}(\varphi)\propto(\chi_{zxx}\sin{\theta}\cos{\varphi}^{2}+(% 2\chi_{xxz}\cot{\theta}^{2}+\chi_{zxx}\cot{\theta}^{2}+\chi_{zzz})\sin{\theta}% ^{3}\sin{\varphi}^{2})^{2},italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_φ ) ∝ ( italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_x italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_sin italic_θ roman_cos italic_φ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + ( 2 italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x italic_x italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_cot italic_θ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_x italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_cot italic_θ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_z italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) roman_sin italic_θ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_sin italic_φ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , (1)
ISHGs⁢(φ)∝(2⁢χx⁢x⁢z⁢sin⁡θ⁢sin⁡φ⁢cos⁡φ)2.proportional-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐼SHG𝑠𝜑superscript2subscript𝜒𝑥𝑥𝑧𝜃𝜑𝜑2I_{\mathrm{SHG}}^{s}(\varphi)\propto(2\chi_{xxz}\sin{\theta}\sin{\varphi}\cos{% \varphi})^{2}.italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_SHG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_φ ) ∝ ( 2 italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x italic_x italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_sin italic_θ roman_sin italic_φ roman_cos italic_φ ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT . (2)

The resulting ratios of the tensor elements in Figure 2b-c, χz⁢z⁢z(2)/χz⁢x⁢x(2)=3.7superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑧𝑧2superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑥𝑥23.7\chi_{zzz}^{(2)}/\chi_{zxx}^{(2)}=3.7italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_z italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT / italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_x italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = 3.7 and χz⁢z⁢z(2)/χx⁢x⁢z(2)=6.5superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑧𝑧𝑧2superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑥𝑥𝑧26.5\chi_{zzz}^{(2)}/\chi_{xxz}^{(2)}=6.5italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z italic_z italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT / italic_χ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_x italic_x italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( 2 ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = 6.5, reflect a thin film under tensile out-of-plane stress[57]. The good quality of the fit curves in Figure 2b-c confirms that our BTO thin film has 4mm point group symmetry. The minor discrepancies between data and fit model might be due to the fact that our BTO thin film is coherently strained to the substrate, and this leads to the appearance of additional minor nonzero tensor elements [58].

Data availability
