The dependence of Tesubscript𝑇𝑒T_{e}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and Tpsubscript𝑇𝑝T_{p}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (averaged over dBTOsubscript𝑑BTOd_{\mathrm{BTO}}italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) on the delay t𝑡titalic_t is reported in Figure S17 for the two incident pump fluences Fin=1.4 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes1.4timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$1.4\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 1.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG and 2.7 mJ cm−2times2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter22.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG and the corresponding fit parameter g𝑔gitalic_g (Table S3), while Figure S22a and Figure S21a report the 2D maps of Tp⁢(z,t)subscript𝑇𝑝𝑧𝑡T_{p}(z,t)italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) as a function of delay t𝑡titalic_t and depth z𝑧zitalic_z at the respective fluences.

Supplementary Note 8 Strain model

The total stress experienced by our sample upon optical laser excitation can be written as [32, 4]:

σ=ρ⁢v2⁢η+σD⁢P⁢(Te,∂Eg∂p)+σT⁢E⁢(Tp,β),𝜎𝜌superscript𝑣2𝜂subscript𝜎𝐷𝑃subscript𝑇𝑒subscript𝐸𝑔𝑝subscript𝜎𝑇𝐸subscript𝑇𝑝𝛽\sigma=\rho v^{2}\eta+\sigma_{DP}\left(T_{e},\frac{\partial E_{g}}{\partial p}% \right)+\sigma_{TE}(T_{p},\beta),italic_σ = italic_ρ italic_v start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η + italic_σ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_D italic_P end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , divide start_ARG ∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_p end_ARG ) + italic_σ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T italic_E end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_β ) , (S11)

where Y=ρ⁢v2𝑌𝜌superscript𝑣2Y=\rho v^{2}italic_Y = italic_ρ italic_v start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT is the Young’s modulus, ρ𝜌\rhoitalic_ρ is the mass density and v𝑣vitalic_v is the longitudinal speed of sound (Table S2). The first term in equation (S11) indicates the direct relationship between the stress σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ, a force that induces a deformation of the material, and the strain η𝜂\etaitalic_η that represents the resulting deformation of the material. The stress σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ causes the generation of a strain wave that propagates through the material and across the interface to the layer below. In equation (S11), ∂Eg/∂psubscript𝐸𝑔𝑝\partial E_{g}/\partial p∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / ∂ italic_p indicates the variation of the bandgap as a function of the electronic pressure, and β𝛽\betaitalic_β is the linear expansion coefficient.

Equation (S11) can be written more explicitly as [32]:

σ⁢(z,t)=ρ⁢v2⁢η⁢(z,t)−ne⁢B⁢∂Eg∂p−3⁢B⁢β⁢(Tp⁢(z,t)−T0),𝜎𝑧𝑡𝜌superscript𝑣2𝜂𝑧𝑡subscript𝑛𝑒𝐵subscript𝐸𝑔𝑝3𝐵𝛽subscript𝑇𝑝𝑧𝑡subscript𝑇0\sigma(z,t)=\rho v^{2}\eta(z,t)-n_{e}B\frac{\partial E_{g}}{\partial p}-3B% \beta(T_{p}(z,t)-T_{0}),italic_σ ( italic_z , italic_t ) = italic_ρ italic_v start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) - italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_B divide start_ARG ∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_p end_ARG - 3 italic_B italic_β ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) - italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) , (S12)

where ne=Ce⁢(Te⁢(z,t)−T0)/(E−Eg)subscript𝑛𝑒subscript𝐶𝑒subscript𝑇𝑒𝑧𝑡subscript𝑇0𝐸subscript𝐸𝑔n_{e}=C_{e}(T_{e}(z,t)-T_{0})/(E-E_{g})italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_C start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) - italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) / ( italic_E - italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) is the carrier density [4] (with unit  m−3timesabsentmeter3\text{\,}{\mathrm{m}}^{-3}start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_m end_ARG start_ARG - 3 end_ARG end_ARG), B𝐵Bitalic_B is the bulk modulus (Table S2), T0=300 Ksubscript𝑇0times300kelvinT_{0}=$300\text{\,}\mathrm{K}$italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 300 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_K end_ARG is the sample temperature at equilibrium, and ne⁢(E−Eg)subscript𝑛𝑒𝐸subscript𝐸𝑔n_{e}(E-E_{g})italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_E - italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) is the total energy density transferred from the optical photons to the electronic subsystem [4]. We consider here only the dependence of Tesubscript𝑇𝑒T_{e}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, Tpsubscript𝑇𝑝T_{p}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and η𝜂\etaitalic_η on the z𝑧zitalic_z direction (sample depth). This one-dimensional approximation is justified by the large ratio between the laser excited area and the BTO thickness, leading to the film contraction/expansion only along the surface normal on the few tens of picoseconds timescale.

The relation between the stress σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ and the atomic displacement u𝑢uitalic_u is described by the following one-dimensional lattice strain wave equation [41]:

ρ⁢∂2u⁢(z,t)∂t2=∂σ⁢(z,t)∂z,𝜌superscript2𝑢𝑧𝑡superscript𝑡2𝜎𝑧𝑡𝑧\rho\frac{\partial^{2}u(z,t)}{\partial t^{2}}=\frac{\partial\sigma(z,t)}{% \partial z},italic_ρ divide start_ARG ∂ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_u ( italic_z , italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_t start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG = divide start_ARG ∂ italic_σ ( italic_z , italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_z end_ARG , (S13)

which can be recast as a function of η⁢(z,t)=∂u⁢(z,t)/∂z𝜂𝑧𝑡𝑢𝑧𝑡𝑧\eta(z,t)=\partial u(z,t)/\partial zitalic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) = ∂ italic_u ( italic_z , italic_t ) / ∂ italic_z:

∂2η⁢(z,t)∂t2=1ρ⁢∂2σ⁢(z,t)∂z2.superscript2𝜂𝑧𝑡superscript𝑡21𝜌superscript2𝜎𝑧𝑡superscript𝑧2\frac{\partial^{2}\eta(z,t)}{\partial t^{2}}=\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial^{2}% \sigma(z,t)}{\partial z^{2}}.divide start_ARG ∂ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_t start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG = divide start_ARG 1 end_ARG start_ARG italic_ρ end_ARG divide start_ARG ∂ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_σ ( italic_z , italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG . (S14)

Substituting equation (S12) in equation (S14) yields:

∂2η⁢(z,t)∂t2=v2⁢∂2η⁢(z,t)∂z2−Ceρ⁢(E−Eg)⁢δ2⁢B⁢∂Eg∂p⁢(Te⁢(z,t)−T0)−3⁢B⁢βρ⁢δ2⁢(Tp⁢(z,t)−T0).superscript2𝜂𝑧𝑡superscript𝑡2superscript𝑣2superscript2𝜂𝑧𝑡superscript𝑧2subscript𝐶𝑒𝜌𝐸subscript𝐸𝑔superscript𝛿2𝐵subscript𝐸𝑔𝑝subscript𝑇𝑒𝑧𝑡subscript𝑇03𝐵𝛽𝜌superscript𝛿2subscript𝑇𝑝𝑧𝑡subscript𝑇0\frac{\partial^{2}\eta(z,t)}{\partial t^{2}}=v^{2}\frac{\partial^{2}\eta(z,t)}% {\partial z^{2}}-\frac{C_{e}}{\rho(E-E_{g})\delta^{2}}B\frac{\partial E_{g}}{% \partial p}(T_{e}(z,t)-T_{0})-\frac{3B\beta}{\rho\delta^{2}}(T_{p}(z,t)-T_{0}).divide start_ARG ∂ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_t start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG = italic_v start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT divide start_ARG ∂ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG - divide start_ARG italic_C start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG italic_ρ ( italic_E - italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) italic_δ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG italic_B divide start_ARG ∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_p end_ARG ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) - italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) - divide start_ARG 3 italic_B italic_β end_ARG start_ARG italic_ρ italic_δ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) - italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) . (S15)

Given the initial conditions [41] η⁢(z,0)=0𝜂𝑧00\eta(z,0)=0italic_η ( italic_z , 0 ) = 0, ∂η/∂t⁢(z,0)=0𝜂𝑡𝑧00\partial\eta/\partial t(z,0)=0∂ italic_η / ∂ italic_t ( italic_z , 0 ) = 0, σ⁢(0,t)=0𝜎0𝑡0\sigma(0,t)=0italic_σ ( 0 , italic_t ) = 0, equation (S15) provides the out-of-plane strain profile η⁢(z,t)𝜂𝑧𝑡\eta(z,t)italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) by solving the following analytical integrals [79]:

η⁢(z,t)={12⁢v⁢∫0t∫z−v⁢(t−τ)z+v⁢(t−τ)𝔉⁢(ξ,τ)⁢𝑑ξ⁢𝑑τ,for t<zv12⁢v⁢∫0t−zv∫v⁢(t−τ)−zz+v⁢(t−τ)𝔉⁢(ξ,τ)⁢𝑑ξ⁢𝑑τ+12⁢v⁢∫t−zvt∫z−v⁢(t−τ)z+v⁢(t−τ)𝔉⁢(ξ,τ)⁢𝑑ξ⁢𝑑τ+𝔉⁢(0,t−z/v)⁢δ2v2,for t>zv,𝜂𝑧𝑡cases12𝑣superscriptsubscript0𝑡superscriptsubscript𝑧𝑣𝑡𝜏𝑧𝑣𝑡𝜏𝔉𝜉𝜏differential-d𝜉differential-d𝜏for t<zv12𝑣superscriptsubscript0𝑡𝑧𝑣superscriptsubscript𝑣𝑡𝜏𝑧𝑧𝑣𝑡𝜏𝔉𝜉𝜏differential-d𝜉differential-d𝜏otherwise12𝑣superscriptsubscript𝑡𝑧𝑣𝑡superscriptsubscript𝑧𝑣𝑡𝜏𝑧𝑣𝑡𝜏𝔉𝜉𝜏differential-d𝜉differential-d𝜏𝔉0𝑡𝑧𝑣superscript𝛿2superscript𝑣2for t>zv\eta(z,t)=\begin{cases}\frac{1}{2v}\int_{0}^{t}\int_{z-v(t-\tau)}^{z+v(t-\tau)% }\mathfrak{F}(\xi,\tau)d\xi d\tau,&\text{for $t<\frac{z}{v}$}\\ \frac{1}{2v}\int_{0}^{t-\frac{z}{v}}\int_{v(t-\tau)-z}^{z+v(t-\tau)}\mathfrak{% F}(\xi,\tau)d\xi d\tau\\ +\frac{1}{2v}\int_{t-\frac{z}{v}}^{t}\int_{z-v(t-\tau)}^{z+v(t-\tau)}\mathfrak% {F}(\xi,\tau)d\xi d\tau+\mathfrak{F}(0,t-z/v)\frac{\delta^{2}}{v^{2}},&\text{% for $t>\frac{z}{v}$},\end{cases}italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) = { start_ROW start_CELL divide start_ARG 1 end_ARG start_ARG 2 italic_v end_ARG ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_t end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z - italic_v ( italic_t - italic_τ ) end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_z + italic_v ( italic_t - italic_τ ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT fraktur_F ( italic_ξ , italic_τ ) italic_d italic_ξ italic_d italic_τ , end_CELL start_CELL for italic_t < divide start_ARG italic_z end_ARG start_ARG italic_v end_ARG end_CELL end_ROW start_ROW start_CELL divide start_ARG 1 end_ARG start_ARG 2 italic_v end_ARG ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_t - divide start_ARG italic_z end_ARG start_ARG italic_v end_ARG end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_v ( italic_t - italic_τ ) - italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_z + italic_v ( italic_t - italic_τ ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT fraktur_F ( italic_ξ , italic_τ ) italic_d italic_ξ italic_d italic_τ end_CELL start_CELL end_CELL end_ROW start_ROW start_CELL + divide start_ARG 1 end_ARG start_ARG 2 italic_v end_ARG ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_t - divide start_ARG italic_z end_ARG start_ARG italic_v end_ARG end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_t end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z - italic_v ( italic_t - italic_τ ) end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_z + italic_v ( italic_t - italic_τ ) end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT fraktur_F ( italic_ξ , italic_τ ) italic_d italic_ξ italic_d italic_τ + fraktur_F ( 0 , italic_t - italic_z / italic_v ) divide start_ARG italic_δ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG italic_v start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG , end_CELL start_CELL for italic_t > divide start_ARG italic_z end_ARG start_ARG italic_v end_ARG , end_CELL end_ROW (S16)

where 𝔉⁢(z,t)=−Ce⁢B⁢∂Eg∂p⁢(Te⁢(z,t)−T0)/[ρ⁢(E−Eg)⁢δ2]−3⁢B⁢β⁢(Tp⁢(z,t)−T0)/(ρ⁢δ2)𝔉𝑧𝑡subscript𝐶𝑒𝐵subscript𝐸𝑔𝑝subscript𝑇𝑒𝑧𝑡subscript𝑇0delimited-[]𝜌𝐸subscript𝐸𝑔superscript𝛿23𝐵𝛽subscript𝑇𝑝𝑧𝑡subscript𝑇0𝜌superscript𝛿2\mathfrak{F}(z,t)=-C_{e}B\frac{\partial E_{g}}{\partial p}(T_{e}(z,t)-T_{0})/[% \rho(E-E_{g})\delta^{2}]-3B\beta(T_{p}(z,t)-T_{0})/(\rho\delta^{2})fraktur_F ( italic_z , italic_t ) = - italic_C start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_B divide start_ARG ∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_p end_ARG ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) - italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) / [ italic_ρ ( italic_E - italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) italic_δ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ] - 3 italic_B italic_β ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) - italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) / ( italic_ρ italic_δ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ). Electron and phonon temperatures, Te⁢(z,t)subscript𝑇𝑒𝑧𝑡T_{e}(z,t)italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) and Tp⁢(z,t)subscript𝑇𝑝𝑧𝑡T_{p}(z,t)italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ), are determined using the 2TM (Supplementary Note 7).

Since the optical pump laser is absorbed mostly in the BTO thin film, but partially also in the SRO layer, there will be two strain waves originating at the vacuum/BTO and the BTO/SRO interfaces. These strain waves propagate through the sample and reflect at each interface, with acoustic reflection coefficients RZsubscript𝑅𝑍R_{Z}italic_R start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_Z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Table S2). The almost identical acoustic impedance of SRO and GSO, leads to a RZ=1%subscript𝑅𝑍percent1R_{Z}=1\%italic_R start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_Z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1 % at the SRO/GSO interface. Conversely, the strain wave undergoes a 13%percent1313\%13 % reflection at the BTO/SRO interface and 100%percent100100\%100 % reflection at the interface with vacuum, given the acoustic impedance Zvacuum=0subscript𝑍vacuum0Z_{\mathrm{vacuum}}=0italic_Z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_vacuum end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0 (Table S2). The superposition of the generated and reflected strain waves yields a total strain wave η⁢(z,t)𝜂𝑧𝑡\eta(z,t)italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ), which is then averaged over the BTO film thickness to obtain η¯⁢(t)=∫0dBTOη⁢(z,t)⁢𝑑z/dBTO¯𝜂𝑡superscriptsubscript0subscript𝑑BTO𝜂𝑧𝑡differential-d𝑧subscript𝑑BTO\overline{\eta}(t)=\int_{0}^{d_{\mathrm{BTO}}}\eta(z,t)dz/d_{\mathrm{BTO}}over¯ start_ARG italic_η end_ARG ( italic_t ) = ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) italic_d italic_z / italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. This quantity is used to fit the experimental average strain data obtained from tr-XRD measurements for Fin=2.7 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$2.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG (Figure 1d) and for Fin=1.4 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes1.4timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$1.4\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 1.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG (Figure S18), with the resulting fit parameters (∂Eg/∂psubscript𝐸𝑔𝑝\partial E_{g}/\partial p∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / ∂ italic_p, β¯T<Tcsubscript¯𝛽𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐\overline{\beta}_{T<T_{c}}over¯ start_ARG italic_β end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and g𝑔gitalic_g) reported in Table S3. The parameter ∂Eg/∂psubscript𝐸𝑔𝑝\partial E_{g}/\partial p∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / ∂ italic_p has been discussed in the main text, we focus here on the discussion of β¯T<Tcsubscript¯𝛽𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐\overline{\beta}_{T<T_{c}}over¯ start_ARG italic_β end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and g𝑔gitalic_g fit results, while the calculations based on our strain model are presented in Supplementary Note 8.

The linear expansion coefficient β¯T<Tcsubscript¯𝛽𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐\overline{\beta}_{T<T_{c}}over¯ start_ARG italic_β end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the fit parameter referring to the average β𝛽\betaitalic_β in the range T0<T<Tcsubscript𝑇0𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐T_{0}<T<T_{c}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, with Curie temperature Tc=400 °Csubscript𝑇𝑐times400celsiusT_{c}=$400\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolCelsius}$italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 400 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG °C end_ARG. While for T>Tc𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐T>T_{c}italic_T > italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, the linear expansion coefficient β𝛽\betaitalic_β is constant and positive (βT>Tc=1.33×10−5 K−1subscript𝛽𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐times1.33E-5kelvin1\beta_{T>T_{c}}=$1.33\text{\times}{10}^{-5}\text{\,}{\mathrm{K}}^{-1}$italic_β start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T > italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG start_ARG 1.33 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG - 5 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_K end_ARG start_ARG - 1 end_ARG end_ARG), it is positive for T0<T<T∗subscript𝑇0𝑇superscript𝑇T_{0}<T<T^{*}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, and negative for T∗<T<Tcsuperscript𝑇𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐T^{*}<T<T_{c}italic_T start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT < italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Figure S4). In our sample, the temperature below Tcsubscript𝑇𝑐T_{c}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT at which β𝛽\betaitalic_β changes sign is T∗=200 °Csuperscript𝑇times200celsiusT^{*}=$200\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolCelsius}$italic_T start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = start_ARG 200 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG °C end_ARG (Figure S4). Based on our model, in the metastable state (t>20 ps𝑡times20picosecondt>$20\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t > start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG), only ≈7 nmabsenttimes7nanometer\approx$7\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$≈ start_ARG 7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG of the BTO film is at T∗<T<Tcsuperscript𝑇𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐T^{*}<T<T_{c}italic_T start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT < italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for Fin=1.4 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes1.4timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$1.4\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 1.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG, while ≈14 nmabsenttimes14nanometer\approx$14\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$≈ start_ARG 14 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG of the BTO film is at T∗<T<Tcsuperscript𝑇𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐T^{*}<T<T_{c}italic_T start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT < italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for Fin=2.7 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$2.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG (Figure S19). As a result, in the latter case the portion of the sample at β<0𝛽0\beta<0italic_β < 0 is larger than in the former case, thus β¯T<Tcsubscript¯𝛽𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐\overline{\beta}_{T<T_{c}}over¯ start_ARG italic_β end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is expected to be smaller, as shown by our fit results (Table S3). At the same time, for both fluences, the larger portion of the sample (≈14−27⁢ nmabsent1427timesabsentnanometer\approx 14-27$\text{\,}\mathrm{nm}$≈ 14 - 27 start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_nm end_ARG) is at T0<T<T∗subscript𝑇0𝑇superscript𝑇T_{0}<T<T^{*}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∗ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, hence β¯T<Tcsubscript¯𝛽𝑇subscript𝑇𝑐\overline{\beta}_{T<T_{c}}over¯ start_ARG italic_β end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_T < italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is expected to be positive, as obtained by our fit results.

The electron-phonon coupling g𝑔gitalic_g of the 2TM (Supplementary Note 7) is a temperature dependent quantity, however for simplicity in our model it is assumed to be constant, similarly to most current investigations of short-pulse laser excitation [80]. In general, the dependence of g𝑔gitalic_g on Tesubscript𝑇𝑒T_{e}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is related to the electron density of states, and, if the d𝑑ditalic_d band electrons are below the Fermi level without crossing it (as in BTO), g𝑔gitalic_g increases with Tesubscript𝑇𝑒T_{e}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. In particular, within the free electron gas model [80], g𝑔gitalic_g is linearly dependent on the electron density nesubscript𝑛𝑒n_{e}italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. This approximation is expected to be valid for relatively low electron temperatures [81], as in our case. In fact, the resulting g𝑔gitalic_g fit parameters (Table S3) scale approximately as the peak electron density given by ne,m⁢a⁢x=Ce⁢(Te,m⁢a⁢x−T0)/(E−Eg)subscript𝑛𝑒𝑚𝑎𝑥subscript𝐶𝑒subscript𝑇𝑒𝑚𝑎𝑥subscript𝑇0𝐸subscript𝐸𝑔n_{e,max}=C_{e}(T_{e,max}-T_{0})/(E-E_{g})italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e , italic_m italic_a italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_C start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e , italic_m italic_a italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) / ( italic_E - italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) [4] (Figure S20). Specifically, ne,m⁢a⁢x=1.7×1027 m−3subscript𝑛𝑒𝑚𝑎𝑥times1.7E+27meter3n_{e,max}=$1.7\text{\times}{10}^{27}\text{\,}{\mathrm{m}}^{-3}$italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e , italic_m italic_a italic_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG start_ARG 1.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG 27 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_m end_ARG start_ARG - 3 end_ARG end_ARG and 3.2×1027 m−3times3.2E+27meter33.2\text{\times}{10}^{27}\text{\,}{\mathrm{m}}^{-3}start_ARG start_ARG 3.2 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG 27 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_m end_ARG start_ARG - 3 end_ARG end_ARG for Fin=1.4 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes1.4timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$1.4\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 1.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG and 2.7 mJ cm−2times2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter22.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG, respectively. For comparison, the electron density in copper, with one electron per atom in the conduction band (Ne=1subscript𝑁𝑒1N_{e}=1italic_N start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1), is neCu=Ne⁢ρ⁢NA/M=8.5×1028 m−3superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑒Cusubscript𝑁𝑒𝜌subscript𝑁𝐴𝑀times8.5E28meter3n_{e}^{\mathrm{Cu}}=N_{e}\rho N_{A}/M=$8.5\text{\times}{10}^{28}\text{\,}{% \mathrm{m}}^{-3}$italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_Cu end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = italic_N start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ρ italic_N start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_A end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / italic_M = start_ARG start_ARG 8.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG 28 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_m end_ARG start_ARG - 3 end_ARG end_ARG, where ρ=8.96 g cm−3𝜌times8.96timesgramcentimeter3\rho=$8.96\text{\,}\mathrm{g}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-3}$italic_ρ = start_ARG 8.96 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_g end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 3 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG is the mass density [82], NA=6.022×1023 atoms/molsubscript𝑁𝐴times6.022E23atomsmolN_{A}=$6.022\text{\times}{10}^{23}\text{\,}\text{atoms}\mathrm{/}\mathrm{mol}$italic_N start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_A end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG start_ARG 6.022 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG 23 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG atoms / roman_mol end_ARG is the Avogadro’s number [83], and M=63.55 g mol−1𝑀times63.55timesgrammole1M=$63.55\text{\,}\mathrm{g}\text{\,}{\mathrm{mol}}^{-1}$italic_M = start_ARG 63.55 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_g end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_mol end_ARG start_ARG - 1 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG is the atomic mass [82]. The absolute value of the fit g𝑔gitalic_g parameters are of similar order of magnitude as for SrRuO3subscriptSrRuO3\mathrm{SrRuO_{3}}roman_SrRuO start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 3 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [37] and other perovskites [84]. Moreover, it is worth noting that the obtained electron densities and the fit g𝑔gitalic_g values are more than one order of magnitude smaller than the corresponding parameters in metals [85, 86, 80], e.g., the electron-phonon coupling in Cu [80] is g≈5×1017 W m−3 K−1𝑔times5E+17timeswattmeter3kelvin1g\approx$5\text{\times}{10}^{17}\text{\,}\mathrm{W}\text{\,}{\mathrm{m}}^{-3}% \text{\,}{\mathrm{K}}^{-1}$italic_g ≈ start_ARG start_ARG 5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG 17 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_W end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_m end_ARG start_ARG - 3 end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_K end_ARG start_ARG - 1 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG.

Supplementary Note 9 Temperature, strain and diffraction curve calculations

Given the fit parameters in Table S3, we calculate the lattice temperature Tp⁢(z,t)subscript𝑇𝑝𝑧𝑡T_{p}(z,t)italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) in the BTO thin film for Fin=2.7 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$2.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG (Figure S21a). Under this condition, 19%percent1919\%19 % of BTO reaches Tcsubscript𝑇𝑐T_{c}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT after 20 pstimes20picosecond20\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG, while for Fin=1.4 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes1.4timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$1.4\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 1.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG the sample remains always below Tcsubscript𝑇𝑐T_{c}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Figure S22a). Hence, a higher pump fluence leads to a larger portion of the BTO reaching higher temperatures and in shorter time. We note that when the pump photon energy E𝐸Eitalic_E is below or ≲0.5 eVless-than-or-similar-toabsenttimes0.5electronvolt\lesssim$0.5\text{\,}\mathrm{eV}$≲ start_ARG 0.5 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_eV end_ARG above the bandgap Egsubscript𝐸𝑔E_{g}italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, peak power intensities in the range from  kW cm−2timesabsenttimeskilowattcentimeter2\text{\,}\mathrm{kW}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_kW end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG to  GW cm−2timesabsenttimesgigawattcentimeter2\text{\,}\mathrm{GW}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_GW end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG lead to a sample temperature increase below 50 Ktimes50kelvin50\text{\,}\mathrm{K}start_ARG 50 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_K end_ARG [12, 14, 15, 16, 19]. In our experiment, given the relatively high peak power intensity in the range 20–38range20382038start_ARG 20 end_ARG – start_ARG 38 end_ARG  GW cm−2timesabsenttimesgigawattcentimeter2\text{\,}\mathrm{GW}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}start_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_GW end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG, and the pump photon energy ≈1.2 eVabsenttimes1.2electronvolt\approx$1.2\text{\,}\mathrm{eV}$≈ start_ARG 1.2 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_eV end_ARG above the bandgap, sample heating is taken into account.

Figure S21b reports the strain map η⁢(z,t)𝜂𝑧𝑡\eta(z,t)italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) in the BTO film based on the results of Tp⁢(z,t)subscript𝑇𝑝𝑧𝑡T_{p}(z,t)italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ) and Te⁢(z,t)subscript𝑇𝑒𝑧𝑡T_{e}(z,t)italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z , italic_t ), with calculation details reported in Supplementary Note 8. Here, it can be clearly seen how the regions of the sample below [above] Tcsubscript𝑇𝑐T_{c}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_c end_POSTSUBSCRIPT display smaller [larger] η⁢(z,t)𝜂𝑧𝑡\eta(z,t)italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ). In particular, for t<3.1 ps𝑡times3.1picosecondt<$3.1\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t < start_ARG 3.1 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG the average strain η¯⁢(t)¯𝜂𝑡\overline{\eta}(t)over¯ start_ARG italic_η end_ARG ( italic_t ) is negative, primarily due to the compressive strain from the deformation potential (Figure 1d). Moreover, the three straight profiles in the Figure S21b mark the front of the strain wave propagating in the BTO at the sound speed and then reflected at the interface with SRO and air.

A few selected strain profiles at fixed delays are highlighted in Figure S21c. Within the first 15 pstimes15picosecond15\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}start_ARG 15 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG after t=0 ps𝑡times0picosecondt=$0\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t = start_ARG 0 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG (Supplementary Note 4) the strain profile η⁢(z,t)𝜂𝑧𝑡\eta(z,t)italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) undergoes relatively large changes resulting from the varying Tesubscript𝑇𝑒T_{e}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and Tpsubscript𝑇𝑝T_{p}italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and the corresponding DP and TE contributions (Figure S23). After ≈15 psabsenttimes15picosecond\approx$15\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$≈ start_ARG 15 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG the strain profile reaches a metastable state at least for the following few tens of picoseconds. The maximum average tensile strain of this metastable state is directly proportional to the incident pump fluence and it depends on the film thickness (Figure S21b-c and Figure S22b-c).

From η⁢(z,t)𝜂𝑧𝑡\eta(z,t)italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ), we calculated c⁢(z,t)=c0⁢[η⁢(z,t)+1]𝑐𝑧𝑡subscript𝑐0delimited-[]𝜂𝑧𝑡1c(z,t)=c_{0}[\eta(z,t)+1]italic_c ( italic_z , italic_t ) = italic_c start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [ italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t ) + 1 ] for each unit cell along z𝑧zitalic_z and subsequently the diffraction profiles in the range 0 ps<t<20 pstimes0picosecond𝑡times20picosecond$0\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$<t<$20\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$start_ARG 0 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG < italic_t < start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG (Figure S21d). A selection of diffraction curves at different t𝑡titalic_t (Figure S21e) are then compared to the experimental data (Figure S21f). The main features of the experimental data, i.e., the shift of the diffraction peak to smaller photon energies, the relative increase/decrease of the diffraction intensity on the low/high photon energy side, the larger broadening and smaller peak diffraction intensity as t𝑡titalic_t increases, are all well reproduced by our simulations. This strongly corroborates the validity of the model employed to describe our data. The initial shift to higher photon energy for t<4 ps𝑡times4picosecondt<$4\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t < start_ARG 4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG is clearly shown by the increase of spectral weight around 1530 eVtimes1530electronvolt1530\text{\,}\mathrm{eV}start_ARG 1530 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_eV end_ARG in Figure S21d. Conversely, for t>4 ps𝑡times4picosecondt>$4\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t > start_ARG 4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG, the average positive strain explains the peak shift to lower photon energy, and the strain gradient is the reason for the change in spectral weight from the high to the low energy side. At the same time, a larger strain gradient leads to broader diffraction curves, and given the conservation of the total area under the curve, to a smaller peak intensity. Qualitatively, the simulated diffraction curves show sharper oscillations, related to the small film thickness, as compared to the experimental ones (Figure S21e-f). This is the consequence of a slightly larger broadening of the experimental IXRDsubscript𝐼XRDI_{\mathrm{XRD}}italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT curves that we assign to an initial strain profile η⁢(z,t=0 ps)≠0𝜂𝑧𝑡times0ps0\eta(z,t=$0\text{\,}\mathrm{p}\mathrm{s}$)\neq 0italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t = start_ARG 0 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG ) ≠ 0, while we assume η⁢(z,t=0 ps)=0𝜂𝑧𝑡times0ps0\eta(z,t=$0\text{\,}\mathrm{p}\mathrm{s}$)=0italic_η ( italic_z , italic_t = start_ARG 0 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG ) = 0 throughout the sample for the simulated IXRDsubscript𝐼XRDI_{\mathrm{XRD}}italic_I start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_XRD end_POSTSUBSCRIPT curves (Figure S24). Finally, the discussion above regarding the incident pump fluence Fin=2.7 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes2.7timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$2.7\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 2.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG applies also to the lower fluence Fin=1.4 mJ cm−2subscript𝐹intimes1.4timesmillijoulecentimeter2F_{\mathrm{in}}=$1.4\text{\,}\mathrm{mJ}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_in end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG 1.4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_mJ end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG. Also in the latter case, we find an excellent agreement between experiments and simulations (Figure S22e-f).

Supplementary Note 10 Estimation of the bandgap decrease

To estimate the largest bandgap decrease, first we determine the photoinduced electronic pressure as Δ⁢P=γe⁢Ce⁢Δ⁢Te≈1.7×109 PaΔ𝑃subscript𝛾𝑒subscript𝐶𝑒Δsubscript𝑇𝑒times1.7E9pascal\Delta P=\gamma_{e}C_{e}\Delta T_{e}\approx$1.7\text{\times}{10}^{9}\text{\,}% \mathrm{Pa}$roman_Δ italic_P = italic_γ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_C start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_Δ italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≈ start_ARG start_ARG 1.7 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG 9 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_Pa end_ARG [4], where γe≈2.6subscript𝛾𝑒2.6\gamma_{e}\approx 2.6italic_γ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≈ 2.6 [87] is the Grüneisen parameter, Cesubscript𝐶𝑒C_{e}italic_C start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the electronic heat capacity (Table S2), and Δ⁢Te=1.56×104 KΔsubscript𝑇𝑒times1.56E4kelvin\Delta T_{e}=$1.56\text{\times}{10}^{4}\text{\,}\mathrm{K}$roman_Δ italic_T start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = start_ARG start_ARG 1.56 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG 4 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_K end_ARG is the largest increase in electronic temperature of the BTO film averaged over dBTOsubscript𝑑BTOd_{\mathrm{BTO}}italic_d start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BTO end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Figure S17). Second, we express the bandgap variation as Δ⁢Eg=∂Eg∂P⁢Δ⁢P≈−3.2 meVΔsubscript𝐸𝑔subscript𝐸𝑔𝑃Δ𝑃times-3.2millielectronvolt\Delta E_{g}=\frac{\partial E_{g}}{\partial P}\Delta P\approx$-3.2\text{\,}% \mathrm{meV}$roman_Δ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = divide start_ARG ∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_P end_ARG roman_Δ italic_P ≈ start_ARG - 3.2 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_meV end_ARG, where ∂Eg∂P=−3.0⁢(5)×10−31 J Pa−1subscript𝐸𝑔𝑃timestimesuncertain-3.0510-31timesjoulepascal1\frac{\partial E_{g}}{\partial P}=$-3.0(5)\text{\times}{10}^{-31}\text{\,}% \mathrm{J}\text{\,}{\mathrm{Pa}}^{-1}$divide start_ARG ∂ italic_E start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_g end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_P end_ARG = start_ARG start_ARG start_ARG - 3.0 end_ARG start_ARG ( 5 ) end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG 10 end_ARG start_ARG - 31 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_J end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_Pa end_ARG start_ARG - 1 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG (Table S3).

Cne=(Δ⁢Psne/Ps,0)/(Δ⁢Psη/Ps,0)subscript𝐶subscript𝑛𝑒Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝑠subscript𝑛𝑒subscript𝑃𝑠0Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝑠𝜂subscript𝑃𝑠0C_{n_{e}}=(\Delta P_{s}^{n_{e}}/P_{s,0})/(\Delta P_{s}^{\eta}/P_{s,0})italic_C start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = ( roman_Δ italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT / italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) / ( roman_Δ italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT / italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ). From polarization-electric field hysteresis loops on differently strained BTO thin films grown on a GSO substrate [26], the relative change of the polarization as a function of the strain was determined to be Δ⁢Psη/Δ⁢η=15 µC/cm2/%\Delta P_{s}^{\eta}/\Delta\eta=$15\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolMicro}\mathrm{% C}\mathrm{/}\mathrm{{}^{2}}\mathrm{c}\mathrm{m}\mathrm{/}\%$roman_Δ italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT / roman_Δ italic_η = start_ARG 15 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_C / start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT roman_cm / % end_ARG. In our data, at short time delays (t<4 ps𝑡times4picosecondt<$4\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t < start_ARG 4 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG) the strain experiences a marginal change Δ⁢η¯<0.02%Δ¯𝜂percent0.02\Delta\overline{\eta}<0.02\%roman_Δ over¯ start_ARG italic_η end_ARG < 0.02 % and Δ⁢PsΔsubscript𝑃𝑠\Delta P_{s}roman_Δ italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is clearly dominated by the presence of photoexcited carriers in the conduction band (Figure 2e-f). At larger time delays (t≈20 ps𝑡times20picosecondt\approx$20\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}$italic_t ≈ start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG), η¯¯𝜂\overline{\eta}over¯ start_ARG italic_η end_ARG reaches the saturation value of 0.34%percent0.340.34\%0.34 %, which would correspond to an increase in polarization of Δ⁢Psη=5.1 µ⁢C cm−2Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝑠𝜂times5.1timesmicrocoulombcentimeter2\Delta P_{s}^{\eta}=$5.1\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolMicro C}\text{\,}{% \mathrm{cm}}^{-2}$roman_Δ italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = start_ARG 5.1 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_C end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG (Δ⁢Psη/Ps,0≈25%Δsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝑠𝜂subscript𝑃𝑠0percent25\Delta P_{s}^{\eta}/P_{s,0}\approx 25\%roman_Δ italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_η end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT / italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≈ 25 %), assuming a polarization at equilibrium of Ps,0≈20 µ⁢C cm−2subscript𝑃𝑠0times20timesmicrocoulombcentimeter2P_{s,0}\approx$20\text{\,}\mathrm{\SIUnitSymbolMicro C}\text{\,}{\mathrm{cm}}^% {-2}$italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≈ start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG start_ARG roman_µ roman_C end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG power start_ARG roman_cm end_ARG start_ARG - 2 end_ARG end_ARG end_ARG as found for BTO/SRO/GSO [26]. In contrast, we measure an overall polarization change of Δ⁢Ps/Ps,0≈−2.5%Δsubscript𝑃𝑠subscript𝑃𝑠0percent2.5\Delta P_{s}/P_{s,0}\approx-2.5\%roman_Δ italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / italic_P start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_s , 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≈ - 2.5 % (Figure 2e). As a result, it follows that the electronic contribution Cnesubscript𝐶subscript𝑛𝑒C_{n_{e}}italic_C start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, responsible for the reduced polarization, has ≈10%absentpercent10\approx 10\%≈ 10 % larger magnitude than the structural one even at 20 pstimes20picosecond20\text{\,}\mathrm{ps}start_ARG 20 end_ARG start_ARG times end_ARG start_ARG roman_ps end_ARG (Cne≈−1.1subscript𝐶subscript𝑛𝑒1.1C_{n_{e}}\approx-1.1italic_C start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_e end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≈ - 1.1).

  • [1] Martin, L. W. & Rappe, A. M. Thin-film ferroelectric materials and their applications. \JournalTitleNat. Rev. Mater. 2, 16087, DOI: 10.1038/natrevmats.2016.87 (2017).

  • [6] Schick, D. et al. Ultrafast lattice response of photoexcited thin films studied by X-ray diffraction. \JournalTitleStruct. dyn. 1, 064501, DOI: 10.1063/1.4901228 (2014).
  • [7] Sheu, Y. M. et al. Using ultrashort optical pulses to couple ferroelectric and ferromagnetic order in an oxide heterostructure. \JournalTitleNat. Commun. 5, 1–6, DOI: 10.1038/ncomms6832 (2014).
  • [8] Lee, H. J. et al. Structural Evidence for Ultrafast Polarization Rotation in Ferroelectric/Dielectric Superlattice Nanodomains. \JournalTitlePhys. Rev. X 11, 031031, DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevX.11.031031 (2021).

  • [22] Yang, Y., Paillard, C., Xu, B. & Bellaiche, L. Photostriction and elasto-optic response in multiferroics and ferroelectrics from first principles. \JournalTitleJ. Phys.: Condens. Matter 30, 073001, DOI: 10.1088/1361-648X/aaa51f (2018).
